root_text.sn47.36:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.36:1.6: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.36:1.8: Imesaṁ kho, bhikkhave, catunnaṁ satipaṭṭhānānaṁ bhāvitattā bahulīkatattā dvinnaṁ phalānaṁ aññataraṁ phalaṁ pāṭikaṅkhaṁ—
root_text.sn47.36:1.9: diṭṭheva dhamme aññā, sati vā upādisese anāgāmitā”ti.

translation_text.sn47.36:1.1: “Mendicants, there are these four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
translation_text.sn47.36:1.3: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.36:1.4: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.36:1.6: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.36:1.7: These are the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
translation_text.sn47.36:1.8: Because of developing and cultivating these four kinds of mindfulness meditation, one of two results can be expected:
translation_text.sn47.36:1.9: enlightenment in this very life, or if there’s something left over, non-return.”
